name: reaver-wps-fork-t6x
summary:
**Reaver** implements a **brute force attack** against **Wifi Protected Setup** (WPS) registrar **PINs** in order to recover **WPA/WPA2 passphrases**, as described in [Brute forcing Wi-Fi Protected Setup When poor design meets poor implementation.](http://sviehb.files.wordpress.com/2011/12/viehboeck_wps.pdf) by **Stefan Viehböck**.  
**Reaver** has been designed to be a robust and practical attack against **Wi-Fi Protected Setup (WPS)** registrar PINs in order to **recover WPA/WPA2 passphrases** and has been tested against a wide variety of access points and WPS implementations.  
**Depending

documentation.readme: Reaver implements a brute force attack against Wifi Protected Setup (WPS) registrar PINs in order to recover WPA/WPA2 passphrases, as described in Brute forcing Wi-Fi Protected Setup When poor design meets poor implementation. by Stefan Viehböck. Reaver has been designed to be a robust and practical attack against Wi-Fi Protected Setup (WPS) registrar PINs in order to recover WPA/WPA2 passphrases and has been tested against a wide variety of access points and WPS implementations. Depending on the target's Access Point (AP), to recover the plain text WPA/WPA2 passphrase the average amount of time for the transitional online brute force method is between 4-10 hours. In practice, it will generally take half this time to guess the correct WPS pin and recover the passphrase. When using the offline attack, if the AP is vulnerable, it may take only a matter of seconds to minutes.
usage_sections: Example: The example uses Kali Linux as the Operating System (OS) as pixiewps is included. You must already have Wiire's Pixiewps installed to perform a pixie dust attack, latest version can be found in its official github repository. - - -

arguments.name: -A
arguments.description: Do not associate with the AP (association must be done by another application)

arguments.name: -a
arguments.description: Show all APs, even those without WPS

arguments.name: --all
arguments.description: Show all APs, even those without WPS

arguments.name: --dh-small
arguments.description: Use small DH keys to improve crack speed

arguments.name: -E
arguments.description: Terminate each WPS session with an EAP FAIL packet

arguments.name: --eap-terminate
arguments.description: Terminate each WPS session with an EAP FAIL packet

arguments.name: --ignore-locks
arguments.description: Ignore locked state reported by the target AP

arguments.name: --json
arguments.description: print extended WPS info as json

arguments.name: -L
arguments.description: Ignore locked state reported by the target AP

arguments.name: -M
arguments.description: Change the last digit of the MAC Address for each pin attempt [False]

arguments.name: --mac-changer
arguments.description: Change the last digit of the MAC Address for each pin attempt [False]

arguments.name: --no-associate
arguments.description: Do not associate with the AP (association must be done by another application)

arguments.name: --no-nacks
arguments.description: Do not send NACK messages when out of order packets are received

arguments.name: -S
arguments.description: Use small DH keys to improve crack speed

arguments.name: -U
arguments.description: Show UTF8 ESSID (does not sanitize ESSID, dangerous)

arguments.name: --utf8
arguments.description: Show UTF8 ESSID (does not sanitize ESSID, dangerous)

arguments.name: -v
arguments.description: Display non-critical warnings (-vv or -vvv for more)

arguments.name: --verbose
arguments.description: Display non-critical warnings (-vv or -vvv for more)

arguments.name: --bssid
arguments.description: Target BSSID (MAC of an access point).

arguments.name: --interface
arguments.description: Network interface to bind to (e.g. wlan0mon, eth0).

arguments.name: --target
arguments.description: Target host, IP, MAC, or URL the tool operates on.

arguments.name: --output
arguments.description: Output file path for the report or capture.
